document:
There’s a rhythm to it. You start with the big stuff—plastic bins stacked too high, the old bike frame leaning in the corner, the shovel with the cracked handle no one’s bothered to fix. Tomas dragged the mower out yesterday so I could get at the back wall, where the sports stuff ended up last winter. A soccer ball, deflated. Three hockey sticks, none of us use anymore. The kids barely skate now.

I sorted what could go to the bin, what might get used, what has to stay. Half those bins were mislabelled anyway. “Lights/Extension Cords” turned out to be holiday clothes. “Tools” was mostly cables and a cracked phone charger. I didn’t sort it right then, just moved things to the middle aisle so I could sweep. Dust, mostly. Mouse droppings near the far corner. Swept that out quick.

The ladder’s back on its hooks. That helped. Feels like a win. I taped up the extension cord now instead of letting it coil on the floor. Less tripping. Noor came out once to drop off a box—“Things from the basement,” she said. I haven’t opened it.

It’s not finished. Not by a long shot. There’s still the gardening tub near the door, cracked and full of dead pots. A bird feeder full of old seed, probably rancid. And the snow blower from last year, still caked in salt and slush residue. I’ll get to it. Maybe Saturday. Or next week.

But the floor’s mostly clear now. You can walk in a straight line from the driveway to the back corner without ducking. That counts. I stood there for a minute earlier, just breathing. No music. No one asking for anything. Just the hum from the fridge in the house next door. Quiet. Not empty, but close. Enough to think or not think. Enough to decide later what to do with the broken step stool. Tomorrow, maybe. Tonight, I just locked up and came inside.

It’s funny how much of your life ends up in a garage. Not the kind of life you post or talk about over drinks—more like the residue. Old shelves with mismatched screws, a bicycle with a slow puncture, boxes marked “kitchen?” in fading pen. I spent most of Saturday trying to sort through it. Not deeply, just enough to open the door without needing a running start.

I found the stroller we used when the kids were small. The fabric’s mildewed at one corner, and one wheel wobbles, but otherwise it’s intact. I stared at it for a minute, trying to recall where we’d last taken it. Maybe the park near the library, the one with the crooked path and the bench that always had gum stuck under it. Can’t be sure.

There’s a stack of records wrapped in plastic. Ingrid gave them to me years ago when she cleaned out her flat—“you play this stuff,” she’d said, though I never really did. They’re all still in their sleeves. I lifted one, blew the dust off the cover. Some French jazz number I don’t recognize. The spine on the jacket is cracked.

Kofi came by mid-afternoon, saw me wrestling with a broken fan and asked if I wanted help. I said no, not really. He nodded like he understood, tossed me a bottle of water, then stood there a moment, looking past me into the dim part where the rakes and ladders lean. “Feels like a museum,” he said. I laughed, but later I wondered—museum of what? Failed projects? Forgotten intentions?

I didn’t finish. Just shifted things around, stacked some boxes higher, threw out a torn tarp. The floor’s a bit clearer, but the same clutter hums underneath—quiet, patient, waiting. I left the light on when I came in. From the kitchen window, it glows like a small, stubborn eye.

The footpaths through the western edge of the neighbourhood follow no formal design. They’re not aligned with the grid of the streets, nor do they adhere to property boundaries. Instead, they cut across corners of yards, skirt the base of retaining walls, and disappear into groves of young maples before reappearing near a cluster of utility poles where cables dip low between arms. These routes emerged gradually. I remember them forming—or at least noticing them—over the span of a few years, though how long they’d been used before that, I can’t say.

They vary in width. Some are barely more than compacted soil, marked only by the lack of vegetation and a subtle trough worn into the surface. Others, like the path that runs parallel to the dry creek bed, are nearly a metre across and edged with uneven flagstones someone must have placed decades ago. The stones are lichen-covered now, slick in the spring. You learn to avoid them or step flat-footed to keep from twisting an ankle.

Amara said she used to walk her dog along one of these paths every morning before work. She doesn’t live here anymore, but while she did, she remarked on the way different people used the same route for different purposes. Runners favoured the smoother stretches, avoiding loose gravel. Parents with strollers had to backtrack where there were drop-offs or gaps in the informal boardwalks made from salvaged plywood. Hana, who walks with a cane, mentioned she only uses the ones with benches, even if they’re longer.

Maintenance, if you can call it that, is unofficial and inconsistent. Someone trimmed back the brambles near the school access point last summer, stacking the cuttings into neat piles that eventually blew into the storm drain. In winter, if snow falls heavily, the routes disappear under drifts and don’t reappear until warm rain or frequent use clears them again. I’ve seen Yuki shove a walking stick into compacted ice to test depth before continuing, though he never complained.

These paths don’t show up on any official map. They’re not lit, signed, or maintained by the city. Still, they’re used—by delivery people on bikes, by teenagers taking shortcuts, by those of us trying to avoid the wide, traffic-heavy avenues. Their alignment suggests a logic based on proximity and convenience, but also on older patterns, perhaps pre-dating the current housing. Foundations of a ruined structure were found one summer when utility crews dug near the slope. Whether that has anything to do with why one path angles sharply near that spot, no one I’ve asked can confirm.

It’s possible they’ll be formalized eventually, paved over or rerouted to accommodate new construction. It’s also possible they’ll persist, silently, used just enough to remain visible, shaped not by planning but by repetition.

I’ve never been good at deciding how I want my coffee—so I keep changing the way I make it. Maybe that says something. The French press sits on the counter like a promise, all heavy glass and brass, impatient for attention. I pull it down, rinse yesterday’s sludge from the bottom, and pour hot water just off the boil. The bloom swells upward, that quiet eruption of trapped gas, and I stir with a wooden spoon we’ve had for years—handle worn down, flecked with old glue from a repair I don’t remember. Ingrid used to laugh when I’d let the plunger drop too fast. "It’s not a race," she’d say, watching the sediment cloud the cup. I still listen for her tone when I lift the pot, strain the last of it into my mug. It feels like something left behind.

Then there’s the espresso maker, that small, stubborn thing on the back corner. It hisses when I crank the portafilter in, and I brace for the gurgle and crema bloom. Luca introduced me to this ritual, slow and deliberate, grinding beans too fine, tamping with too much force until the machine complained. He’d stand there, arms crossed, waiting for it to sing at the end. Now I do it the same way, pressing too hard, mostly out of habit. The shot comes out thick and bitter, just right. I don’t even add sugar anymore. It’s sharp in the mouth, like the things we didn’t say.

But most mornings, I use the old drip machine. It’s the one Devon gave me before they moved north, a no-name model with a cracked carafe and a switch that clicks only if you nudge it just right. It still works, though. I measure the grounds—too much, always too much—tamp them down lightly, and let the water crawl through. The drip, drip, drip starts slow, then steadies. That sound is my alarm clock now. I stand there, barefoot on the cold floor, watching the pot fill. It’s weak coffee, not the best, but it’s easy. It doesn’t ask anything of me.

I wonder sometimes if it’s the coffee I’m after or just the motion—the water, the heat, the small acts that mean nothing and everything. I carry the cup to the window. Outside, the streetlights are fading. A neighbor’s dog barks. I lift the cup, blow across the surface, and think about nothing at all.

Reorganising the garage started out as a way to make space for the lawnmower, which had been leaning at a tilt against a stack of old plant pots since early spring. But once the door was fully open and the light reached the back corners, it became clear the project was less about the mower and more about everything else—items accumulated in no particular order over years, each with its own small claim to usefulness. A plastic crate of holiday lights, tangled and still in their December shapes. A child’s bicycle with a deflated rear tire. A collection of paint tins, some open, some not, their labels faded or peeled.

I began by pulling everything into the yard, stacking in rough categories: garden, tools, seasonal, uncertain. Noor helped for an hour in the morning, moving the heavier bins, but left after her phone rang and she muttered something about a leak. The work slowed after that. Sorting through a box marked “misc. hardware” revealed a set of keys with no known lock, a hinge with screws missing, and a small bundle of wires tied in a knot. It wasn’t junk, not exactly, but its utility was questionable. Still, I set it aside—potential value outweighed the space it took up.

Shelving was installed along the left wall using brackets that matched the concrete’s uneven texture. Not all anchors seated properly, but after adjusting the depth on two units, they held. The workbench, previously buried under stacked boxes, was cleared and given a new position near the power outlet. It now hosts the drill, the sander, and a tin of bits, each in their own compartment. Not perfectly, but visibly separated.

Painting wasn’t planned, but the wall behind the workbench showed stains from oil and weather. A single coat of primer-grey helped, though the patch remains obvious under direct light. It’s functional. The hose reel was relocated from the side of the house, freeing up space and reducing trip hazards near the door.

Some things were discarded—broken flowerpots, a cracked storage tub, an old pair of gloves hardened by sun exposure. Others were boxed for later decisions: holiday decorations, camping gear not used in two seasons, a bag of fabric scraps Ruth left behind last summer. They sit by the driveway, waiting for transport.

There’s still a zone near the ceiling where items are stored out of reach, held up by cords and brackets. Not accessible, not entirely forgotten. The feeling is less of completion and more of temporary containment. The mower now fits, upright, near the door. That was the goal, technically met.
